metadata.summary: Event-sourced aggregate root pattern: apply/replay cycle, command handlers, from_events reconstruction, optimistic concurrency, and pending-event collection.

content.title: Event Sourcing — Aggregate Root Pattern
content.summary: The event-sourced aggregate root reconstructs its state by replaying a list of past events and emits new events (never mutating state directly) when commands succeed. State MUST be mutated only inside apply() handlers, never inside command methods.

why.p: The aggregate root is the consistency boundary. All invariant checks happen here before any event is emitted. By separating "command executes invariant check + emits event" from "apply(event) mutates state", the aggregate becomes trivially testable via given/when/then and fully replayable from any point in history. Mixing state mutation into command methods breaks replay determinism.

list.item: Implementing an event-sourced domain model that has business invariants to enforce.
Any aggregate root in a CQRS system where commands are handled and events are persisted.
When you need to reconstruct aggregate state from a repository (load events, replay, then execute command).
Simple CRUD entities with no invariants — the apply/replay overhead adds zero value.
Cross-aggregate operations — never load two aggregates and mutate both in one command handler; use sagas/process managers instead.

rule.statement: Aggregate state MUST be mutated only inside _apply (or apply) event handlers, never inside command methods. Command methods validate invariants, emit events, and return.
rule.rationale: Replay calls apply() on historical events without going through command logic. If command methods mutate state directly, replay produces different state than the original command execution — the entire point of event sourcing is broken.

rule.statement: Repository MUST call save(stream_id, events, expected_version) with the version loaded at the start of the command. Omitting expected_version causes silent ghost writes on concurrent commands.
rule.rationale: Without optimistic concurrency, two concurrent commands loading the same aggregate version both succeed; the second write overwrites the first event stream silently. expected_version makes this a detectable conflict.

rule.statement: The aggregate MUST expose a collect_pending_events() method that returns and clears the list of new events. The repository calls this after save() succeeds.
rule.rationale: Pending events drive outbox publishing, projection updates, and saga triggers. They must be collected after persistence, not during command execution, to avoid double-publishing on retry.

example.title: Event-sourced Order aggregate (Python)
example.note: State mutation lives ONLY in _on_* handlers. Command methods (add_item, place, ship) validate invariants then call _apply. from_events replays history with is_new=False so no pending events are collected.

antipattern.description: Mutating aggregate state directly inside command methods instead of inside apply handlers.
antipattern.reason: Agents commonly write self.status = "placed" in command methods because it feels natural. This breaks replay: replaying events skips the command path but must reproduce the same state. State mutation belongs in _on_* handlers only.

antipattern.description: Loading multiple aggregates (Customer, Inventory, Payment) in a single command handler and mutating all of them.
antipattern.reason: Cross-aggregate mutations are not atomic across event streams. This creates ghost states and eventual consistency violations. Use a saga or process manager to coordinate across aggregates.
Without optimistic concurrency, concurrent commands at the same version both succeed. The second write silently overwrites the first, producing incorrect state with no detectable error.

section.name: testing-pattern
section.p: The canonical aggregate test shape is given/when/then: given a list of past events (replay state), when a command is executed, then a specific list of new events is emitted. This directly tests the aggregate's event emission logic without touching the repository or store.

example.title: Given/when/then aggregate test (Python)
example.code:
from uuid import uuid4 from decimal import Decimal import pytest
def test_add_item_to_draft_order(): order_id = uuid4() customer_id = uuid4() product_id = uuid4()
# given order = Order.from_events(order_id, [ OrderCreated(order_id=order_id, customer_id=customer_id), ])
# when order.add_item( product_id=product_id, product_name="Widget", quantity=2, unit_price=Decimal("9.99"), )
# then events = order.collect_pending_events() assert len(events) == 1 assert isinstance(events[0], OrderItemAdded) assert events[0].product_id == product_id assert events[0].quantity == 2
def test_cannot_add_item_to_placed_order(): order_id = uuid4() customer_id = uuid4()
order = Order.from_events(order_id, [ OrderCreated(order_id=order_id, customer_id=customer_id), OrderPlaced(order_id=order_id, shipping_address={}, total_amount=Decimal("0")), ])
with pytest.raises(DomainError, match="Cannot modify placed order"): order.add_item(uuid4(), "Widget", 1, Decimal("1.00"))
example.note: No database, no repository — just aggregate state reconstruction from events and assertion on emitted events.
